1.description: Event history. Panel A plots red zone events as captured by business debt growth and equity price growth along with the advent of financial crises as defined by Baron, Verner, and Xiong (2021). Panel B presents a similar plot with red zone events defined using household debt growth and house price growth. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

2.description: Crisis prediction on expanding sample. This figureВ presents theОі$\gamma$-coefficient from our main three-year crisis prediction model when we iteratively test the model on an expanding sample starting inT=$T=$1990 and ending in 2012. The left figureВ of each panel presents the results from the univariate regression modelCrisisi,t+1tot+3=ai+ОіГ—R-Zoneit+Оµit$ Crisis_{i,t+1\text{ to }t+3} =a_i + \gamma \times {\it R{\hbox{-}}Zone}_{it} +\epsilon _{it}$. The right figureВ in each panel presents theОі$\gamma$-coefficient from the multivariate regression modelCrisisi,t+1tot+3=ai+ОІГ—HighDebtGrowthit+ОґГ—HighPriceGrowthit+ОіГ—R-Zoneit+Оµit$ Crisis_{i,t+1\text{ to }t+3} =a_i +\beta \times {\it High \ Debt \ Growth}_{it} +\delta \times {\it High \ Price \ Growth}_{it} + \gamma \times {\it R{\hbox{-}}Zone}_{it} +\epsilon _{it}$.Crisisi,t+1tot+3${\it Crisis}_{i,t+1 \text{ to } t+3}$is an indicator variable equal to one if a crisis has occurred in countryi$i$within three years of timet$t$.HighDebtGrowthв‰Ў1{О”3(DebtPriv/GDP)it>80thpercentile}${\it High \ Debt \ Growth}\equiv 1\lbrace \Delta _3 (Debt^{Priv}/GDP)_{it}>80^{\text{th}} \text{ percentile}\rbrace$is an indicator variable equal to one if three-year debt growth is in the highest quintile of our full sample, whileHighPriceGrowthв‰Ў1{О”3log(Priceit)>66.7thpercentile}${\it High \ Price \ Growth}\equiv 1{\lbrace \Delta _3 \log (Price_{it})>66.7^{\text{th}} \text{ percentile}\rbrace }$is an indicator variable equal to one if three-year price growth is in its highest tercile of our full sample. TheR-Zonevariable is the intersection of high price growth and high debt growth:R-Zoneв‰ЎHighDebtGrowthГ—HighPriceGrowth${\it R{\hbox{-}}Zone}\equiv {\it High \ Debt \ Growth}\times {\it High \ Price \ Growth}$. We run the regressions on both the business sector, using business debt and equity prices to define the indicators (Panel A), and the household sector, using household debt and house prices to define the indicators (Panel B). Ninety-five percent confidence intervals are calculated using Driscoll and Kraay (1998) standard errors with five lags and Kiefer and Vogelsang (2005) fixed-b$b$asymptotics. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

3.description: Fraction of countries in R-Zone. The figureВ depicts the fraction of countries in the red zone at a given time,GlobalR-Zonetв‰Ў1Ntв€‘iв€€StR-Zoneit$ {\it Global \ R{\hbox{-}}Zone}_t\equiv \frac{1}{N_t}\sum _{i\in S_t} {\it R{\hbox{-}}Zone}_{it}$, whereNt$N_t$is the number of countries in our sample at timet$t$andSt$S_t$is the set of countries in the sample at timet$t$. We calculateGlobalR-Zonet${\it Global\ R{\hbox{-}}Zone}_t$for each sector, that is, using business debt growth paired with equity price growth and using household debt growth paired with house price growth, to defineR-Zoneit${\it R{\hbox{-}}Zone}_{it}$. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

4.description: GDP growth following red zone events. This figureВ presents the empirical distribution of (annualized) GDP growth over horizons of one to four years following a red zone event (either business or household) versus the empirical distribution of (annualized) GDP growth following country-years not in the red zone. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

5.description: Empirical policy possibility frontier. Panel A presents the optimal combinations of precision (the percentage ofR-Zonefollowed by a crisis) and sensitivity (percentage of crises preceded by aR-Zone) attainable by varying the thresholds for entering theR-Zone. Panel B presents the optimal combinations of specificity (percentage of noncrisis years not preceded by aR-Zone) and sensitivity (percentage of crises preceded by aR-Zone) attainable by varying the thresholds for entering theR-Zone. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

6.description: Financial crises in and out of the red zone. The figureВ presents all crises and their severity plotted against the debt and price growth percentiles of the year closest to the red zone in the three years leading up to the crisis. The red zone is shaded area in the top right of the figure, and we measure how close each country-year-sector is to the red zone with the Euclidian distance of percentiles:max(0.8в€’debtgrowthpercentile,0)2+max(2/3в€’pricegrowthpercentile,0)2$\sqrt {\max (0.8-\text{debt growth percentile},0)^2+\max (2/3-\text{price growth percentile},0)^2}$. We measure the severity of a crisis as the three-year real (log) GDP growth following the crisis. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

7.description: Policy possibility frontier. This figureВ plots the policy possibility frontier,П„TNR=TTNR(П„TPR)$\tau _{TNR}=T_{TNR}(\tau _{TPR})$, in(П„TPR,П„TNR)$(\tau _{TPR},\tau _{TNR})$space together with policymakers' linear indifference curves, which take the formIndifferenceв€’CurveTNR(П„TPR)=Constв€’p1в€’puвЂІ(YВЇL)uвЂІ(YВЇH)cFNcFPГ—П„TPR$ {\textit{Indifference-Curve}}_{TNR}(\tau _{TPR})=Const-\frac{p}{1-p}\frac{u^{\prime } (\bar{Y}_L)}{u^{\prime } (\bar{Y}_H)}\frac{c_{FN}}{c_{FP}}\times \tau _{TPR}$. At the optimal value ofП„TPR$\tau _{TPR}$, the slope of the policy possibility frontier is equal to the slope of the indifference curve. Panel A illustrates these trade-offs for an initial position of the policy possibility frontier. The solid red curve shows a case in whichCFN/CFP$C_{FN}/C_{FP}$is low, leading to a low level ofП„TPRв€—$\tau ^*_{TPR}$. The dashed red curve shows a case in whichCFN/CFP$C_{FN}/C_{FP}$is high, leading to a high level ofП„TPRв€—$\tau ^*_{TPR}$. Panel B illustrates how the trade-off changes when crises become more predictable, leading to an outward shift in the policy possibility frontier. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

8.description: Model calibration. This figureВ shows the model solution for optimal test sensitivity (П„TPRв€—$\tau _{TPR}^*$) as we varycFP$c_{FP}$/cFN$c_{FN}$. Recall thatcFP$c_{FP}$/cFN$c_{FN}$is the ratio of two macroeconomic treatment effects. Specifically, conditional on the risk of a crisis truly being high,cFN$c_{FN}$is the expected percentage increase in the present value of future real output given a policy action to lean against the wind relative to the baseline level of output absent that policy action. Similarly,cFP$c_{FP}$gives the expected percentage decline in the present value of real output from taking the same policy action when risk is truly low. We assumep=4%,uвЂІ(YВЇB)/uвЂІ(YВЇG)=1$p=4\%, u^{\prime } (\bar{Y}_B )/u^{\prime } (\bar{Y}_G)=1$,CCrisis/YG=1.5$C_{Crisis}/Y_{G}=1.5$. Thus, for each value ofcFP$c_{FP}$/cFN$c_{FN}$, we report the solution toTTNRвЂІ(П„TPR)пё·Slopeofpolicypossibilityfrontier=в€’p1в€’pГ—uвЂІ(YВЇB)uвЂІ(YВЇG)Г—CCrisisYGГ—CFNCFPпё·Slopeofpolicyindifferencecurves=в€’0.040.96Г—1Г—1.5Г—CFNCFP$\mathop{\overbrace{{T}_{\textit{TNR}}^{{}^{\prime}}({\tau}_{\textit{TPR}})}}\limits^{\text{Slope}\hspace*{0.2424242424242424em}\text{of}\hspace*{0.2424242424242424em}\text{policy}\hspace*{0.2424242424242424em}\text{possibility}\hspace*{0.2424242424242424em}\text{frontier}}=\mathop{\overbrace{-\frac{p}{1-p}\ensuremath{\times{}}\frac{{u}^{\prime}({\bar{Y}}_{B})}{{u}^{\prime}({\bar{Y}}_{G})}\ensuremath{\times{}}\frac{{C}_{\textit{Cris}\textit{is}}}{{Y}_{G}}\ensuremath{\times{}}\frac{{C}_{\textit{FN}}}{{C}_{\textit{FP}}}}}\limits^{\text{Slope}\hspace*{0.2424242424242424em}\text{of}\hspace*{0.2424242424242424em}\text{policy}\hspace*{0.2424242424242424em}\text{indifference}\hspace*{0.2424242424242424em}\text{curves}}=-\frac{0.04}{0.96}\ensuremath{\times{}}1\ensuremath{\times{}}1.5\ensuremath{\times{}}\frac{{C}_{\textit{FN}}}{{C}_{\textit{FP}}}$. To estimateTTNRвЂІ(П„TPR)$ T_{TNR}^{^{\prime }} (\tau _{TPR} )$, we first estimateTTPR(П„TPR)$ T_{TPR} (\tau _{TPR} )$parametrically using nonlinear least squares, generating a smoothed version of our empirical policy possibility frontier. We use the empirical frontier from the right-most column of TableIV, Panel B, which combines information from the business and household sectors. (Recall that our raw empirical policy possibility frontier plots the true negative rateвЂ”the fraction of noncrisis years that are not preceded by a red zone event in the prior three yearsвЂ”as a function of the true positive rateвЂ”the fraction of crisis years preceded by a red zone event in the prior three years.) More specifically, we assume thatTTPR(П„TPR)=1в€’О¦((О¦в€’1(П„TPR)в€’a)/b)$ T_{TPR} (\tau _{TPR} ) =1- \Phi ((\Phi ^{-1} (\tau _{TPR} ) -a )/b )$, whereО¦(В·)$ \Phi (\cdot )$is the standard normal cumulative distribution function. We obtaina=0.95$ a=0.95$andb=0.85$ b=0.85$withR2=99.96%$ \nobreakspace R^{2}=99.96\%$. We then obtainTTNRвЂІ(П„TPR)=в€’(1/b)Г—[П•((О¦в€’1(П„TPR)в€’a)/b)]Г·[П•(О¦в€’1(П„TPR))]$ T_{TNR}^{^{\prime }} (\tau _{TPR} ) = - (1/b ) \times [ \phi ((\Phi ^{-1} (\tau _{TPR} ) -a )/b ) ] \div [ \phi (\Phi ^{-1} (\tau _{TPR} ) ) ]$. Using this estimate ofTTNRвЂІ(П„TPR)$ T_{TNR}^{^{\prime }} (\tau _{TPR} )$, we report the solutionП„TPR*$ \tau _{TPR}^{\ast }$as we varycFP/cFN$ c_{FP}/c_{FN}$from 0 to 70. We also report the positive predicted valuePPV(П„TPR*)$ PPV (\tau _{TPR}^{\ast } )$вЂ”the fraction of red zone events that are followed by the onset of a crisis within three yearsвЂ”corresponding to the optimal test sensitivity. To do so, we use nonlinear least squares to fit a truncated fourth-order polynomial to the empirical plot ofPPVversusTPR:PPV(П„TPR)=min{1,a+bВ·(П„TPR)+cВ·(П„TPR)2+dВ·(П„TPR)3+eВ·(П„TPR)4}$PPV (\tau _{TPR} ) =\min \lbrace 1,a+b \cdot (\tau _{TPR} ) +c \cdot (\tau _{TPR} ) ^{2} +d \cdot (\tau _{TPR} ) ^{3}+e \cdot (\tau _{TPR} ) ^{4} \rbrace $which givesR2=99.92%$ R^{2}=99.92\%$. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
